generations.text:
**To My Dearest Elara,**

**April 12th, 1892**

I write this with the ink smudged and my hands shaking, for the wind howls like a wounded beast through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea roars, and I can feel the walls trembling beneath me. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn, but if I do, I must speak these words to you.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not heard from you since. The silence has grown heavier with each passing day, like the weight of the sea pressing against these stones. I have tried, Elara, I have tried to reach you, to call you home, but you have turned your back on me, and I have not the heart to beg.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, of the light that guides the lost. But you were my light, my only light, and I fear I have lost you to the darkness of your own choosing. You left without a word, without a farewell, as if I were a stranger, a ghost of the past. I do not know what you saw in me that made you flee, but I wish I could have been the father you needed, the man you wanted.

The storm is coming. I have seen the sky turn the color of blood, and the wind has begun to howl like a beast in

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea roiling as if it knows the hour is late. It is past midnight, and the storm is upon us with a fury that makes my bones ache. I have stood guard for many years, but never have I felt so alone as I do now, with the weight of the world pressing upon my shoulders and the silence of your absence echoing in my heart.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left this house, that once was our home, and now stands empty, save for the wind and the waves. I have tried to keep the light burning, not just for the ships that sail the sea, but for the hope that one day you might return. Yet, the light has dimmed in my heart, and the beacon that once guided you home now casts its glow upon an empty shore.

I remember the last time we spoke. You were so angry, so full of words that cut deeper than any blade. I had not seen you in months, and when I finally did, you stood there, a stranger in my own home, your eyes filled with tears that would not fall. You said you were tired of the loneliness, of the silence, of the way I never seemed to understand you. I tried to explain, to apologize, but my voice faltered, and I could not find the words. And

**[A rough, ink-stained letter, the paper trembling slightly in the flickering light of the lighthouse lamp. The handwriting is jagged, as though the pen is being wrestled by the writer's hand.]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside, the sea clawing at the cliffs with a fury I’ve never known. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say what I should have said long ago.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left. Five years since you turned your back on this lighthouse, on me, on the life we once had. I’ve been alone here, with only the lamp, the sea, and the ghosts of our past.

You were always so quick to leave, weren’t you? You never stayed to listen. You never stayed to understand. You thought I was too old, too stubborn, too much of a relic. You said I was a fool to stay here, that I was clinging to a life that had long since passed. But I stayed. Because I had to. Because this lighthouse was my duty, my purpose. And I would not abandon it, even if the world had forgotten me.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the sea roars like a beast in pain, I find myself unable to sleep

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I write to you now, with the wind howling like a banshee and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very heavens. The storm is upon us, and I do not know if I shall see another dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this—before the waves claim me, before the light fades from my eyes, I must speak.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I have not spoken your name in so long, and yet it lingers in my thoughts like the ghost of a lighthouse beam on a foggy night. I have been a fool, I know. A man who let pride and silence turn his daughter into a shadow in the corner of his mind. I have not written, I have not called, and I have let the years pass like the tides—unseen, unremembered, and unfeeling.

But now, as the storm gathers, I see the truth. You were not the one who left. It was I. I let fear and shame keep me from you. I thought that if I did not speak, I would not hurt you again. But I was wrong. You were always here, waiting, longing, while I stood at the edge of the world, too afraid to turn back.

I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to its whispers, and I have come

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old lighthouse. The sea is rising, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, as though the heavens themselves are weeping. I fear I may not see the dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not see you again.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of silence that has grown heavier with each passing day. I’ve tried to forget, but the memories cling to me like barnacles on the hull of a ship. I remember the day you left—your face pale, your eyes full of something I could not name. You said you needed to find yourself, and I said I would wait. I did not know then that you would not return.

I have kept the light burning, as I always have, for you. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have whispered your name into the wind. I have told myself that one day, you would come back. But time has worn away the edges of my hope, and I fear that hope is now but a distant echo.

This storm is unlike any I’ve seen in my years here. The sea is rising, and the wind is tearing at the shutters. I have lit the lamp, and I will keep it lit, not just for the ships that pass, but for the memory of a daughter who once believed

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The night is dark, the wind howls like the wails of lost souls, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath me. I fear the storm may take more than the lighthouse—though I would not trade this place for the world, even now.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and I have not heard from you in that time. I have often wondered whether you still remember me, or if time has worn away the memory of your father, the man who once held your hand as you first stepped onto the island, who taught you the stars and the tides, and who tried, in his clumsy way, to be the kind of father you deserved.

I have kept the lighthouse as you once wished me to, though the years have taken their toll. The light still shines, though not as brightly as it once did. The lantern is old, the oil is thin, and the wind threatens to extinguish it. But I will not let it go out—not while I still have breath in my body.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks and imagined your laughter among them. I have told myself that perhaps, in time, you would return. But time has been unkind, and I fear I may not live to see the

**The Lighthouse, Cliffton Point**  
*October 12th, 1887*  

My Dearest Eliza,  

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that gathers on the horizon. Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not ceased its ceaseless rhythm, my soul has grown still, as if the waves themselves have grown tired of my weeping. I have not spoken to you in this time, and I fear I may never again. But tonight—tonight, as the wind howls and the sky darkens with fury—I feel the need to speak, to be heard, even if you do not answer.  

The storm is upon us, Eliza. The sea, that ever-faithful companion, has grown restless. The waves have risen, and the wind, that ancient beast, howls with a voice like the wail of a thousand lost souls. I have stood at the helm for hours, watching the sky turn to a shade of black that even the moon cannot pierce. The lighthouse, my life's work, my only tether to the world, stands as it always has—unyielding, steadfast. But I am not so certain of my own strength.  

I have always believed that the sea is a cruel mistress, and tonight, I fear she may take me. I have spent my life in her service, and I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay your heart may be heavy with the weight of our silence. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and though I have often thought of you, the distance between us has grown like the sea between these cliffs and your distant town. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the certainty that I may not live to see another dawn.

The storm is upon us, a tempest of such fury that even the sea, so accustomed to the wrath of the skies, seems to tremble. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the waves rise like mountains, crashing against the rocks with a sound that shakes the very bones of this lighthouse. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many a night, though tonight it feels more like a farewell than a beacon.

You were always a bright light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the days when you would sit by the fire, your eyes wide with wonder as I told you of the stars and the sea. You had a gift for poetry, and I often wished that you might have taken the path of the poet rather than the merchant’s daughter. But life, as I have come to know it, is not always kind to dreams.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if you will read this. Perhaps

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since grown calloused from the weight of silence. Five years have passed since you last set foot in this lighthouse, and though I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, I have not ceased to think of you. The sea has always been a cruel mistress, but I fear it may yet be more cruel to me than it has ever been.

Tonight, the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves have grown restless, as if they sense the storm that is coming. The lantern burns with a steady light, as it always has, but I cannot shake the feeling that this night may be the last time I shall see the sea with my own eyes. The weather has turned foul, and I have not seen a soul in these parts for days. The last ship passed through yesterday, and I have not heard from them since.

You were always a curious child, Eleanor. You would ask questions that no one else dared to voice, and you would look at the sea not with fear, but with wonder. I remember the day you first climbed the stairs to the lantern room. You were no more than ten, and you stood there, wide-eyed and breathless, as if the world had just been revealed to you in its entirety. I told you then that the sea is a fickle thing, and that it takes what

**April 12th, 1897**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. I have written it in haste, for the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the sky itself. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as I sit here in the lighthouse, I feel your absence more keenly than ever.

I know you would not believe me if I told you how often I think of you. Not just in the quiet moments, when the world is still and the only sound is the whisper of the tide, but in the chaos of the storm, when the wind is a living thing and the sea is a beast that must be tamed. I have always been a man of duty, of routine, of watching the horizon and ensuring the light burns bright. But I have never been able to keep the light burning for you, not truly.

You left me with a broken heart and a world that seemed to shrink in the wake of your departure. I remember the night you walked out—your back turned, your hands clenched at your sides, your eyes like distant stars that would never return. You said you needed space, that you couldn’t stay, that you didn’t know how to be a daughter to a man who had spent his life alone. I didn’t argue. I didn’t try to hold

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of the storm already upon the horizon, the wind howling like the ghost of my own sorrow. The sea, as ever, is restless, and I fear that the waves may claim me before the dawn. Yet, I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I am a man who has spent his life watching the sea, and yet I have not watched the sea as I have watched the silence between us.

You were a child when you left, and I was a man who thought himself strong enough to hold you close. But you were not content with the quiet of our home, the steady light of the lighthouse, the rhythm of the tides. You longed for the world beyond, for the cities and the crowds, for the life that I could not give you. And I, in my pride and my fear, did not understand. I thought that if I kept the light burning, if I kept the lamp clean and the lens clear, I would keep you safe. But I was wrong.

You were not safe in the lighthouse, and neither was I in the silence that followed your departure. I have not spoken your name in five years, and I have not written to you, not even once. I told myself that you would come back, that you would return when the storm passed and the sea calmed. But the storms have come and gone, and you

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea has been restless all day, and the sky is a mass of black clouds, thick as the shadows that have grown between us. I fear the storm may claim me before dawn, and if it does, I hope this letter finds you, and that you may read these words with a heart not yet hardened by time and silence.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not been kind to either of us. I have kept the lighthouse, as you would have expected, but it is a lonely duty. The light still burns, though I have not seen a soul to greet it since the day you left. I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and I know that the silence has been as heavy on you as it has on me. I do not know if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if you have forgotten the way I used to call you “my little lark.”

I have not written to you, and I have not sent you a single letter, for I feared what I might say. I feared that I would speak too softly, or too harshly, or perhaps too much at all. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I have spent these years in the company of the sea, and

**April 2nd, 1887**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear the silence between us has grown heavier than the sea air that fills this lighthouse. I write now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a man who has spent too many nights staring into the darkness, wondering if he has ever truly been seen.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, the way the wind howls like a beast with no mercy. The sea is restless tonight, and I have seen the sky turn the color of old blood. I do not know if I will survive this night, and if I do, I do not know what I will be left to say. But I must write, Eleanor. I must say the things I have kept buried in my chest for too long.

You were twelve when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday—the sky was grey, the sea was calm, and your eyes were filled with a fire I could not understand. You stood at the door of this lighthouse, your father’s coat too large for your small frame, and you said, “I must go, Father.” I did not argue. I did not try to hold you back. I only watched as you walked away, the wind pulling

My Dearest Clara,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, with the storm upon us, I find myself unable to hold my tongue.

You were just a girl when you left, and I was a fool not to see the sorrow in your eyes. I thought I could shield you from the world, but I was wrong. You had a heart too wide for the narrow path I thought best for you. You wanted more than the quiet life of a lighthouse keeper’s daughter. You wanted the world, and I did not understand that. I thought I was protecting you, but I was only keeping you from the life you were meant to live.

I have spent these years alone, watching the waves and the stars, and I have come to understand that I was wrong. I was not the father you needed, and I was not the man you deserved. I have been selfish, and I have been blind. I have not spoken to you because I feared I would say the wrong thing, or worse, that I would be too much for you to bear.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the light must be kept burning, I feel the weight of my silence. I have not been there for you, and I fear I may never be again. I have

**[Sealed with a wax imprint of a broken compass]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, the sea clawing at the rocks below with a fury that would make even the most hardened sailor weep. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have not spoken your name in all that time, not even to myself. But tonight, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the light burns ever brighter, I find I cannot hold back the truth any longer.

You were a child when you left, a child who believed the world was a place of endless possibilities. I was a man who believed in duty, in the light, in the silent promise that I would keep the beacon burning for all who sailed the dark waters. But you saw only the shadow I cast, the man who never gave you the world you dreamed of, who never held you close enough to feel safe.

You said I was a monster, that I had no heart, that I had never loved you. And perhaps you were right. I did not love you as a father should. I loved the sea, the light, the duty. I loved the silence of the night, the solitude of the tower, the weight of responsibility on my shoulders. I did not love you, not in the

*My Dearest Eleanor,*

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, the wind howling like the devil himself has taken up residence in the tower. The sea roars in protest, and the sky is a bruised shade of black, heavy with the weight of the storm that is upon us. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and if it does, I hope you will forgive me for the silence that has stretched between us for these five long years.

I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not spoken to anyone else since. The lighthouse has been my only companion, and the sea my only confidant. I have watched the waves for years, and in their endless rhythm, I have found a kind of peace. But it is a peace that does not extend to you, my daughter.

I know I have been cruel, and I know I have been wrong. I was too proud, too stubborn, too blind to see the pain I caused you. You were a child then, and I was a man who believed that the world was a place of reason and order, and that love could be measured in duty and discipline. I was wrong. I should have seen you, not as a burden, but as a gift. I should have listened when you begged to stay, when you begged to be understood.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have watched the dawn break over

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea rages, and the sky is black as the void between stars. I do not know if I shall live to see dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I fear I shall never speak to you again.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw you. Five years of silence, of unanswered letters, of memories that cling to me like salt on the skin. I have tried to forget, to pretend that the bitterness in my heart was not there. But the sea has a way of reminding us of things we'd rather not remember.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with fire in her eyes and a heart too brave for her years. I told you I could not keep you here, that the lighthouse was not a place for children. You said you understood. You said you would return when you were older. But you never came back.

I have watched the years pass, and with them, the hope that you might one day return. I have stood at the top of the tower, the light burning bright through the storm, and I have wondered if you ever saw it from the other side. If you ever thought of me at all.

I have not spoken of you to the others, not even to the few who remain. The other keepers have long since left, and

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you this evening, with a heart both heavy and full, for I find myself at the mercy of the sea and the storm that now gathers in the east. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves, as if in some cruel jest, rise higher with each passing hour. I have lit the lantern, as I always do, though I fear it may not be enough to guide the weary souls who sail these waters tonight.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if the memories of our last parting have faded like the wax of an old candle. I know you have your own life to live, and perhaps I was too stern, too rigid in my ways, in those days when you chose to leave. But I must tell you now, in this final hour, that I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to pray for your soul.

The sea has always been my companion, my burden, and my solace. I have watched the stars and the moon, and I have seen the dawn break over the cliffs with a quiet dignity that I have come to admire. But tonight, as I stand alone in this tower, I find myself longing for the warmth of a hearth, the laughter of a child, and the presence of someone who knows me, who remembers me.

I have been thinking of you, El

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same bitter cold and solitude that I have known for years. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I suppose it is no surprise, for you have always been a woman of your own will, and I have been a man of my own stubbornness. We both made our choices, and neither of us has looked back.

But tonight—tonight is different. The sea is restless, the sky is bruised with dark clouds, and the wind howls as if it were the voice of the damned. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, but I do not know if I will be here to see the dawn. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can speak these words to you.

I have thought of you often, my dear. Not with the bitterness that once filled my heart, but with a sorrow that has settled deep in my bones. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have wondered if they are like the tears I have shed in the night, unbidden and unrepentant. I have wondered if you think of me at all, or if you have forgotten the man who once held you in his arms, who once gave you his heart without hesitation.

You were always the daughter I wished for—

**[Sealed with a wax emblem of a lighthouse and a single tear]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write to you from the edge of the world, where the sea howls like a beast in pain and the sky weeps with the weight of a thousand years. The wind now sings a mournful dirge through the cracks of this old tower, and I fear the storm that approaches shall not be the only thing to claim my life. Yet, I must write—though I know you may not read these words, and though I fear that even if you do, you may not believe them.**

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not heard a word from you, not a single letter, not even a whisper of your well-being. I have kept the lamp burning, as I always have, for the safety of those who sail upon these waters. I have tended to the light, as I have tended to the silence between us. You have not returned to visit, nor have you written, and I have not dared to write, for fear that I might say something that would only widen the chasm between us.

I do not know why you left, nor do I know if you still hold any love for me. I have tried to forgive, but forgiveness is a bitter thing, and I have found it hard to swallow. I have tried to forget, but the memory of your last visit lingers like

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. The sea has been restless these past few days, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and though the beacon burns bright, I cannot shake the feeling that tonight will be different. The storm is coming, and I have no certainty that I shall see dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have tried to move on, I have not. The lighthouse stands tall, as it always has, but it is not the same without your laughter echoing in the wind. I have often thought of you, Eleanor, and the days we spent together—when the sea was calm and the stars shone like scattered diamonds upon the waves. You had a way of making even the darkest nights feel lighter, and I miss that more than I can say.

I do not write this letter with the hope of reconciliation, for I know that words, like the sea, can be fickle. You have your own life, your own path, and I have no right to expect you to return to this place, this life, or to me. But I must say this: I have never stopped loving you. Not once. And I pray that, in your heart, you may still remember the man who once held you in his arms and promised you the world.

I have prepared for the storm, as

**April 12th, 1897**  
**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. The sea has been restless this week, and the wind has taken on a voice that sounds like a warning. I've been keeping the light burning all night, though the fog has been so thick I can barely see the rocks below. Tonight, the storm is coming—great, rolling clouds that have been gathering on the horizon like a gathering army. I can feel it in my bones, and I fear it may not be kind to any of us.

You were right, my girl. I have been a fool. I should have come to you when you needed me, not waited for you to come to me. I’ve spent the last five years haunted by the silence of your letters, the weight of your words, and the ache of your absence. I have been a lighthouse keeper, yes, but I have also been a man who has lost his way. I’ve kept the light burning for others, but I have not kept it for myself.

I know you think I have changed, that you have moved on, that your heart has found peace in a world without me. But I have not moved on, Eleanor. I have not been able to let go. Every time I see the sea, I hear your voice. Every time I light the lamp, I feel your presence in the flick

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee at my door, and the sea roaring as if it seeks to swallow the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, in this hour of uncertainty, I find myself compelled to write, not to beg for your return, but to say what I have long kept locked within my heart.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. The silence between us has grown as deep and unyielding as the sea. I have watched the stars from this tower, and I have wondered if you still do the same, or if you have turned your face from the sky. I have tended the light, as I have always done, and I have kept the flame burning, not for the ships that pass, but for the hope that one day, you might return.

You were once the light in my life, Eleanor. I remember the day you came to visit, the way the sun broke through the clouds and kissed your face, and the way you laughed as you climbed the steps of the tower. You were full of fire and spirit, and I, a man of stone and salt, was proud to call you my daughter. You taught me that even the most stubborn of men can be softened by love, and I, in my foolishness, let it slip through

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the lighthouse walls. The sea roars with a fury I have never known, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, as the storm brews, I find myself thinking of you—of the girl I once knew, and the woman I have lost.

Five years have passed since you left, and though the world may have moved on, I have not. The lighthouse remains my prison, my sanctuary, my burden. I still stand at the window each morning, watching the horizon, as if expecting to see your face in the mist. I still light the lamp, though the beam no longer guides you home.

I have not written to you, nor have I spoken, for I feared what words might do. You were always so proud, so headstrong, and I feared that any letter I sent would be met with silence, or worse, with a rejection I could not bear. But now, as I face the end of this cruel, unrelenting night, I find I cannot bear the silence any longer.

You were right to leave, Eleanor. I was not the father you needed. I was too stubborn, too rigid, too wrapped up in the duties of this lonely post. I let the sea take precedence over your dreams, your ambitions, your life. I let the lamp burn brighter than your heart. And for that

**To My Dearest Clara,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**Lighthouse, Point Hallow, Isle of Wight**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that brews on the horizon. The sea howls like a wounded beast, and the wind screams as if it knows the truth I’ve buried for five long years. I’ve not spoken your name in such a time, and yet, as the waves crash against these stones, I find myself whispering it to the wind, hoping it might carry it to you.

You were only ten when you left. A child, yet you chose to walk away from your father, from the life we built together. I’ve tried to forget, to let time mend what you could not. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the sea rises, I feel the weight of that choice press upon me like the very cliffs that guard this lighthouse.

I’ve kept the lamp lit, as I always have. It is my duty, my purpose. But tonight, I fear the lamp may not be enough to keep the darkness at bay—neither the darkness of the sea nor the darkness within me. I’ve not spoken to you in five years, Clara, but I’ve not forgotten you. I’ve not stopped watching for the light, nor for the day you might return.

You were my light, my daughter, my greatest joy. I remember the nights

My Dearest Eliza,

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a wounded beast, and I find myself reaching for the pen with trembling hands. It is the same storm that has haunted my nights for years, the one that has driven me to the edge of despair, and now it returns, as if to remind me of all that I have lost.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have I heard from you. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried with all the strength I have, but the silence between us has grown thicker than the fog that curls around these rocks. I have been a lighthouse keeper, yes, but I have also been a father, and I have felt the weight of your absence like the sea's pull on the shore.

I write to you now, not with the hope that you will read it, but with the hope that you might one day understand.
